behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: The core failure—HMAC validation logic, signature comparison—is deterministic in-process computation. A mocked queue is sufficient to verify the non-call when validation fails. HMAC-SHA256 validation is a crypto library operation with no I/O or concurrency risk. The only concern is HTTP response code generation, which is synchronous logic.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with real HMAC-SHA256 crypto library and mocked queue. Construct a request with intentionally invalid signature, invoke handler, verify handler returns 401 status and queue.enqueue() was never called (using call verification on the mock).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mock queue is sufficient because the failure mode is purely about the handler's decision logic: does it skip the enqueue? A mocked queue can verify this via call counting. No external I/O or durability concern.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: The word 'persistent' is key—a real failure is when the response is sent before the enqueue call completes or persists. A mock queue that returns instantly will hide the bug where the handler sends the response before awaiting the real persistent queue. Testing requires a real in-process queue (e.g., SQLite, file-backed queue, or genuine in-memory store with durability semantics) to verify both the ordering and the persistence guarantee. Mocks cannot reliably detect if the handler is written to fire-and-forget enqueues without awaiting.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real in-process persistent queue. Instrument both the queue write and HTTP response methods to record execution timestamps and order. Send a valid webhook and verify: (1) queue.enqueue() is called, (2) enqueue completes or persists, and (3) response is sent after enqueue call initiated. Use instrumentation or mock callbacks with strict ordering assertions.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real queue required because failure mode is about durability: if the handler doesn't actually persist the event before responding, and crashes before the write flushes, the event is lost. A mock doesn't expose this risk.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: A fast mock of downstream processing will silently pass a test even if the handler incorrectly awaits the downstream task. Real failure: handler blocks on slow downstream. To catch this, downstream must actually be slow (either a real slow service or a mock with explicit delays/sleeps). A test that doesn't introduce realistic downstream latency won't expose blocking behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real async downstream processing (or mock configured to sleep). Send a webhook, set downstream processing to introduce a 1–2 second delay, measure the HTTP response time, verify it completes in <100ms (or well before downstream finishes). Assert that response is sent while the downstream task is still running.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If downstream is mocked to return instantly, the test will pass regardless of whether the handler awaits it. Real async concurrency (or slow mock) is required to detect blocking.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a concurrency and contention test. A single synchronous request with a slow mock will pass trivially. Real failure: multiple concurrent requests cause head-of-line blocking if the handler improperly waits on earlier requests' downstream work. Testing requires real async concurrency (spawning multiple requests in parallel) and realistic downstream latency. Concurrency bugs are invisible in single-request tests.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real async concurrency. Send 10–15 webhooks concurrently, configure downstream processing to sleep 2 seconds per request, measure wall-clock response time for each request. Verify all responses arrive within 3 seconds. Time the slowest response to confirm it does not grow with the number of concurrent requests.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A mock that returns instantly or a single-request test will hide concurrency bugs. Multiple real concurrent requests with realistic downstream delays are required to expose blocking under load.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication requires an ID store (in-memory or persistent). If the ID store is mocked or purely in-memory (not persisted), a real failure—restarting the service and resubmitting the same ID—will not be caught. The word 'persistent' in the context of queues suggests the service is designed for durability across restarts, so the ID store should also be persistent. Testing requires a real in-process durable ID store (database, cache with persistence, or file-backed store).
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real persistent ID store. (1) Send webhook with event ID='abc', verify queue.enqueue() called once. (2) Send identical webhook with same ID='abc', verify queue.enqueue() is not called a second time. (3) Send webhook with different ID='def', verify it is enqueued. Assert deduplication works and is ID-specific.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: In-memory-only ID tracking will hide bugs where the service forgets duplicate IDs after restart. Real persistent storage required to catch this failure mode.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: The failure is the handler's error handling logic: does it return 503 + Retry-After when the queue is full? The queue can be mocked to simulate the 'full' condition. Response code generation is synchronous, deterministic logic with no I/O dependency. The handler's error path is straightforward conditional logic.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with mocked queue configured to throw or return a 'queue full' error. Send a webhook, intercept the queue.enqueue() call, trigger the 'full' condition, verify handler catches the error and returns 503 status with Retry-After header set to a valid retry interval.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mock is sufficient because the failure being tested is the handler's response to an error code from the queue. The queue's actual capacity enforcement is a separate concern (behavior 7).

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This tests the queue implementation itself, not just the handler's error handling. A mock queue will always succeed or always fail—it won't enforce a real capacity limit. To verify enforcement, the test must interact with the actual queue and observe that it rejects writes beyond the limit. If the queue is in-process (local data structure or file-backed), Integration is sufficient. If the queue is external (Redis, RabbitMQ, PostgreSQL), System is needed. Assume in-process persistent queue.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real in-process persistent queue configured with capacity=N. (1) Enqueue N-1 events via the handler, verify all succeed. (2) Attempt to enqueue the Nth event to reach capacity. (3) Attempt to enqueue event N+1, verify the queue rejects it. (4) Clear one event, attempt enqueue again, verify it now succeeds, confirming capacity is enforced.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mock queue cannot enforce real capacity limits. Real queue implementation required to detect bugs where capacity validation is missing or incorrectly implemented.
